summary: DHH argues that the tech industry thrives on selling unnecessary complexity to people who equate sophistication with value. He contends that vendors exploit insecurities—fear, vanity, pride—to convince buyers that simple, ownable solutions are beneath them, when in reality most problems don't require expensive, subscription-based tools. The post frames this as a psychological trap that people can break free from simply by choosing to. He closes by encouraging readers to embrace simplicity across their tech stack.
key_points: People claim to want simplicity but consistently buy complexity because it makes them feel special
The tech industry manufactures fear and insecurity to sell subscription-based solutions to basic problems
The shift from ownership to rentals (subscriptions) is a deliberate strategy to extract ongoing revenue
The complexity spell only works as long as you believe in it—opting out is always within your power
money_quotes: It's hard to sell simple, because simple looks easy, and who wants to pay for that?
Everyone says they want something simple, but the way they buy reveals that they usually don't.
Few people have the courage to admit their life and work isn't that complicated.
Basic problems people could easily solve for themselves, cheaply and quickly, getting turned into scary and insurmountable challenges that only a sophisticated solution will cure.
They'll own nothing and you'll convince them they should be happy.
By what sorcery? Fear, mostly. Vanity, sometimes. Sloth, occasionally. Pride, definitely.
The spell only binds as long as you choose to believe in it.
key_insight: The tech industry profits by making simple problems look complex, exploiting insecurity to sell sophisticated subscriptions for what people could easily solve themselves.
